figures.caption: Figure 1: Multi-Scale Separable Fourier Neural Networks (MS-SFNN) architecture diagram. This architecture employs variable separation: each input dimension xj is processed independently by a dedicated single-hidden-layer network. Each subnetwork applies a linear map, followed by a dimension-specific scaling factor ρj and a cosine activation. The M -dimensional outputs from all subnetworks are combined via element-wise multiplication to form the basis functions. The final solution is a linear combination of these bases.
Figure 2: Heat maps illustrate the heat conduction equation (21) with k = 20π. Left: the exact solution; Middle: the prediction solution of MS-SFNN; Right: the absolute error between them.
Figure 3: Heat maps illustrate the heat conduction equation (21) with k = 100π. Left: the exact solution; Middle: the prediction solution of MS-SFNN; Right: the absolute error between them.
Figure 4: Heat maps illustrate the heat conduction equation (21) with k = 500π. Left: the exact solution; Middle: the prediction solution of MS-SFNN; Right: the absolute error between them.
Figure 5: Heat maps illustrate the two-dimensional Helmholtz equation (22) with k = 24π. Left: the exact solution; Middle: the prediction solution of MS-SFNN; Right: the absolute error between them.
Figure 6: Heat maps illustrate the two-dimensional Helmholtz equation (22) with k = 48π. Left: the exact solution; Middle: the prediction solution of MS-SFNN; Right: the absolute error between them.

Figure 7: Heat maps illustrate the two-dimensional complex geometry Helmholtz equation with k = 24π. Left: the exact solution; Middle: the prediction solution of MS-SFNN; Right: the absolute error between them.

Figure 8: Heat maps illustrate the two-dimensional complex geometry Helmholtz equation with k = 48π. Left: the exact solution; Middle: the prediction solution of MS-SFNN; Right: the absolute error between them.
Figure 9: Heat maps illustrate the complex geometry Poisson equation (23). Left: the exact solution; Middle: the prediction solution of MS-SFNN; Right: the absolute error between them.

Figure 11: Heat maps illustrate the three-dimensional Helmholtz equation (25) with z = 0.1. Left: the exact solution; Middle: the prediction solution of MS-SFNN; Right: the absolute error between them.
Figure 12: Heat maps illustrate the three-dimensional Helmholtz equation (25). Left: the exact solution; Middle: the prediction solution of MS-SFNN; Right: the absolute error between them.

Figure 13: Heat maps illustrate the two-dimensional flower-shaped problems with Dirichlet boundary (26) with νfreq = 1000Hz. Left: the exact solution; Middle: the prediction solution of MS-SFNN; Right: the absolute error between them.

Figure 14: Heat maps illustrate the two-dimensional flower-shaped problems with Dirichlet boundary (26) with νfreq = 10000Hz. Left: the exact solution; Middle: the prediction solution of MS-SFNN; Right: the absolute error between them.

Figure 15: Heat maps illustrate the two-dimensional flower-shaped problems with mixed boundary (27) with νfreq = 1000Hz. Left: the exact solution; Middle: the prediction solution of MS-SFNN; Right: the absolute error between them.

Figure 16: Heat maps illustrate the two-dimensional flower-shaped problems with mixed boundary (27) with νfreq = 10000Hz. Left: the exact solution; Middle: the prediction solution of MS-SFNN; Right: the absolute error between them.
Figure 17: Heat maps illustrate the nonlinear elliptic equation (28). Left: the exact solution; Middle: the prediction solution of MS-SFNN; Right: the absolute error between them.
Figure 18: Heat maps illustrate the u component of the nonlinear elliptic equation (28). Left: the exact solution; Middle: the prediction solution of MS-SFNN; Right: the absolute error between them.
Figure 19: Heat maps illustrate the v component of the nonlinear elliptic equation (28). Left: the exact solution; Middle: the prediction solution of MS-SFNN; Right: the absolute error between them.
Figure 20: Heat maps illustrate the pressure p of the nonlinear elliptic equation (28). Left: the exact solution; Middle: the prediction solution of MS-SFNN; Right: the absolute error between them.

Figure 21: Heat maps illustrate the u component of the double-cylinder steady NavierStokes equations (32) with p is chosen as case 1. Left: the exact solution; Middle: the prediction solution of MS-SFNN; Right: the absolute error between them.

Figure 22: Heat maps illustrate the v component of the double-cylinder steady NavierStokes equations (32) with p is chosen as case 1. Left: the exact solution; Middle: the prediction solution of MS-SFNN; Right: the absolute error between them.

Figure 23: Heat maps illustrate the u component of the double-cylinder steady NavierStokes equations (32) with p is chosen as case 2. Left: the exact solution; Middle: the prediction solution of MS-SFNN; Right: the absolute error between them.

Figure 24: Heat maps illustrate the v component of the double-cylinder steady NavierStokes equations (32) with p is chosen as case 2. Left: the exact solution; Middle: the prediction solution of MS-SFNN; Right: the absolute error between them.

Figure 25: Heat maps illustrate the p component of the double-cylinder steady NavierStokes equations (32) with p is chosen as case 2. Left: the exact solution; Middle: the prediction solution of MS-SFNN; Right: the absolute error between them.

Figure 26: Left: the exact solution is selected as case 1. Right: the exact solution is selected as case 2. The heat map illustrates the variation of the L∞ error of u with respect to ρ1 and ρ2, where ρ1 and ρ2 each range from 1 to 200. The colorbar is plotted on a logarithmic scale.
